text:
Admission Date:  1984-05-04              Discharge Date:   1978-09-23

Date of Birth:  1992-10-19             Sex:   M

Service: CARDIOTHORACIC

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Jillian
Chief Complaint:
chest pain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
cardiac catheterization
CABG x4

History of Present Illness:
75 y.o. man with hx of labile HTN presents to Suburban Hospital after +
stress test.  Patient says that 2-3 weeks ago he noticed a
"strange sensation" in his chest after lifting several heavy
boxes and carrying them down a flight of stairs.  Pain
dissipated with rest and patient thought nothing of it.  A week
prior to admission, he was walking down a street with his
daughter at faster than his usual pace when he noticed slight
bilateral chest "pain and pressure" in his upper chest which
again was relieved with rest.  No nausea or diphoresis with the
pain.  The next day, he again experienced the pain while walking
and made an appointment with his PCP.  Randall Johnson saw his PCP Henry 05-15
with CC of chest pain.  He ordered a stress test for 2019-05-20.
Stress test revealed anginal symtoms on exercise with 3 mm
ST-segment depressions on ECG.  Nuclear portion was positive for
a large, reversible, severe perfusion defect involving the LAD
territory, a small, reversible, mild perfusion defect involving
the PDA territory, transient cavity dilation, consistent with
multi-vessel disease, and mild left ventricular systolic
dysfunction with apical and distal inferior hypokinesis,
consistent with stunning.  Patient was transported directly to
the cath lab.  ECG done at cath lab was normal and patient's
cath was deferred until the next morning.
.
Patient says that he is healthy at baseline.  He does all his
own housekeeping including laundry, shopping, and vaccuming with
problem.  Phillip Elliott orthopnea, PND, or pedal edema.  Able to walk up
and down the 2 flight of stairs in his house many times without
difficulty.


Past Medical History:
HYPERTENSION, LABILE
Hx of SCARLET FEVER
DIVERTICULITIS
KIDNEY STONE
S/P REMOVAL OF APPENDIX
S/P LEFT HERNIA REPAIR
S/P B/L KNEE REPLACEMENT FOR OA
PROSTATE CANCER, s/p brachitherapy in 1976
corneal transplant

Social History:
Widowed, lives alone in 5085 Marisa Points
Lake Sheilachester, OH 92426 and [**State 108**] during the winter.
Retired mechanical engineer. Three children: 1 in 934 Kirby Mount
Christinaton, MA 03703, 1
in CA, 1 in NY. Quit tobacco in '[**80**] after 15- 25 pk-yrs. Social
ETOH, usu wine with dinner. Denies illicit/IVDA hx. No regular
exercise.

Family History:
Father deceased of MI age 75. Mother had hx of HTN, died of "old
age" at 90.  Sister with breast CA age 45. No FH of DM.

Physical Exam:
VS: 97.3 137/59 81 18 99%
GEN: well-groomed, well-nourished pleasant elderly man in NAD
HEENT: AT, NC, EOMI, no LAD, no bruits, unable to assess JVD
CV: RRR, nl s1, s2, no m/r/g
PULM: CTAB, no w/r/r
ABD: soft, round, non-tender, non-distended, no HSM, + BS
EXT: no c/c/e, +2 DP BL
NEURO: alert, oriented, CN II-XII grossly intact

Pertinent Results:

Stress:
 4.5 minutes on Modified David, resting HR: 60, BP: 160/90,
Peak: HR: 115, 79% of predicted, BP: 150/80
Symptoms during exercise: Angina
Reason exercise terminated: Hypotensive response
ECG findings: 3 mm ST-segment depression
.
Nuclear:
1. Reversible, large, severe perfusion defect involving the LAD
territory.
2. Reversible, small, mild perfusion defect involving the PDA
territory.
3. Transient cavity dilation, consistent with multi-vessel
disease.
4. Mild left ventricular systolic dysfunction with apical and
distal inferior hypokinesis, consistent with stunning.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient underwent cardiac catheterization on hospital day #2
which showed 3 vessel disease that was most amenable to
treatment via surgical revascularization. Pre-op workup showed
patent carotic arteries bilaterally with less than 40% stenosis.
Echocardiography revealed mild symmetric left ventricular
hypertrophy with normal cavity size, mild dilation of the aortic
root, moderate dilation of the descending aorta, mildly
thickened aortic valve leaflets with mild aortic regurgitation,
mildly thickened mitral valve leaflets with mitral
regurgitation, and mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension.
Ejection fraction was normal.
The patient was taken to the operating room on 1994-10-02 where he
underwent CABGx4. He tolerated this procedure well.
Post-operatively, he was taken to the CSRU. He was extubated
that night. On post-op day 1, the swan line was changed over
wire to a triple lumen catheter. The patient required insulin,
lidocaine and nitro drips overnight, and these were weaned over
the course of the first post-operative day. The patient was
transferred to the floor on post-op day #1. He was seen by the
physical therapy service and ambulated regularly during his
post-op course. On post-op day #2, his chest tube was removed.
On post-op day #3, the pacing wires were removed and discharge
planning was begun. That night, the patient developed a 30
minute run of rapid atrial fibrillation which was treated with
IV lopressor and then IV amiodarone. The patient was stabilized
on PO amiodarone and was asymptomatic thereafter. On post-op day
#4, the cardiology service was contaced, and plans were arranged
for the patient to follow up with Dr. Huff after discharge.
Discharged to home with VNA services in stable condition on POD
#5. Pt alert and oriented, 106/77, RR20, P 78 SR 95% RA sat,
incisions C/D/I.

Hypertension
hx scarlet fever
renal calculi
diverticulitis
s/p corneal transplant
s/p bilat. total knee replacements for OA


Discharge Condition:
Stable


Henryge Instructions:
Please return to the hospital or call Dr. Huff [**Last Name (Prefixes) 2546**] office
if you experience chills or fever greater than 101 degrees F.
Please call if you notice redness, swelling, or tenderness of
your chest wound, or if it begins to drain pus.
No heavy lifting or driving until follow up with surgeon.
Shower, wash incision with mild soap and water and pat dry.

Followup Instructions:
Please follow up with Dr. Huff in 4 weeks. Call for an
appointment. Follow up with Dr. Huff in 2 weeks. Call for
an appointment. Follow up with Dr. Watkins in 2 weeks.
